title: Vanilla fudge
subtitle: Beaten fudge with a soft, short crumb
excerpt: Classic beaten fudge made by cooking cream, sugar, and butter to soft ball stage, then beating the cooled mixture until it turns opaque, grainy, and sets to the characteristic short, crumbly texture. Vanilla throughout.
sourceNotes: Fudge is an American invention documented from the late 19th century; the earliest printed recipes date from around 1886 and are associated with American women's colleges. British fudge developed from American recipes in the early 20th century and is now a standard British confection, particularly associated with seaside and holiday towns. The defining technique is the beaten fudge method: cooking to soft ball stage, then beating vigorously to encourage crystallisation. It is documented in period American and British confectionery guides.

content.text: Fudge is cooked sugar that has been encouraged to crystallise into tiny grains, producing a smooth but short, crumbly texture. The cooking stage is straightforward: heat to

and stop. The harder stage is the beating: the mixture must be beaten vigorously and continuously once it has cooled to around 60°C to initiate crystallisation. It will be liquid, then thick, then suddenly turn opaque and set in the bowl. Pour it into the tin immediately at that point.

Line a 20 cm square tin with baking paper. Place the sugar, cream, and butter in a medium heavy-based saucepan. Heat gently, stirring, until the sugar dissolves and the butter melts. Increase the heat to medium and cook without stirring, clipping a sugar thermometer to the side, until the mixture reaches 116°C. Remove from the heat immediately.

Add the vanilla and salt. Leave the saucepan undisturbed to cool until the mixture reaches 60°C on the thermometer, about 20 minutes. Do not stir during this time. Once at 60°C, beat vigorously with a wooden spoon or electric hand mixer. The mixture will gradually thicken, lose its gloss, and turn opaque and matte. This can take 5 to 10 minutes of sustained beating. As soon as the fudge holds its shape when the spoon is lifted and looks grainy and set, pour immediately into the lined tin and spread level. Leave to set at room temperature for 2 hours before cutting into squares.
